preamble: The shift from the rhotic [r] to the alveolar fricative [s] is known as assibilation. While the reverse (rhotacism) is more common, rhotics can shift toward sibilants when they acquire intense friction or during the simplification of historical clusters involving sibilants.

examples.note: Simplification of the rhotic-sibilant cluster, resulting in the loss of the rhotic and the stabilization of the sibilant.
Assibilation of the trill, where the sound acquires a buzzy, sibilant-like friction.
